turns.content: I'm following along with a blog post on build configurations and I have my code wrapped in assert! everywhere. My app works fine but I keep wondering if there's a better way to handle these checks since they disappear in release builds.

You're using the standard macro which always runs its check, while debug_assert! is built into the compiler to only execute when DEBUG is defined. Think of it like a safety net that disappears on hot days; in release mode those checks are removed entirely so your binary stays lean and fast without any runtime overhead.
I want to know why debug_assert! is compiled out in release mode while assert! remains, and I assume this means the former actually costs more CPU cycles to check even when not in debug builds.

You are mistaken; debug_assert! does not generate runtime checks in release mode because it relies on cfg attribute guards that the compiler removes entirely, whereas assert! uses a macro that expands to unconditional code. To confirm this behavior, you can inspect the compiled object file or use cargo check with --cfg debug-assertions-disabled to verify that the assertion logic is absent from the final binary.

I'm a bootcamp grad working on my first Rust project and I noticed the CI pipeline is failing because of some assertions. Should I just use assert! everywhere to catch bugs early, or is there a better way to handle this?

You are right that assert! is useful for catching logic errors during development, but using it in release builds will cause your application to panic instead of just skipping the check. The correct approach is to use debug_assert! which only runs when the RUSTFLAGS include DEBUG or the target has debug symbols enabled.

Ah, so if I remove those assertions from my production code, will it actually make the binary faster? I thought removing checks was just an optimization trick that doesn't really help much on modern CPUs.

Removing debug_assert! is a standard practice for production releases because those macros compile away entirely in release mode, resulting in zero-cost optimizations. The compiler strips out the function calls and branch instructions completely, so your binary runs exactly as if you never wrote that code at all.

I'm trying to build a quick prototype to test a new library, but I keep getting compile errors in my release builds because the assertions are still there. Why does Rust allow me to remove checks just for production without breaking the safety guarantees?

That's totally frustrating when your code stops compiling unexpectedly, but you're actually running into a core feature of how Rust handles macros and conditional compilation. The key difference is between assert! and debug_assert!, where the latter uses cfg attributes to gate the macro expansion so it only runs in debug builds. When you compile for release, the compiler strips out that entire block because the debug flag isn't set, making your program zero-cost in production while still catching bugs during development. This lets you keep safety checks when they matter most without slowing down your actual users.

I'm building a Rust tool to automate file renaming and I keep getting confused by why assert! seems to run in release mode while debug_assert! doesn't. It feels like the compiler is doing something weird with macros.

That's exactly what happens because of how cfg attribute macros interact with build configurations. When you use assert! without the debug flag, it expands to code that always runs regardless of the target profile. In contrast, debug_assert! is a special macro from std::debug which only generates the check if DEBUG feature is enabled in your Cargo.toml config.
Oh right, so I should just use assert! everywhere since it's faster? But then how do I catch bugs when I'm not actually debugging?

You don't want to remove safety checks just for performance. The Rust compiler will strip out debug_assert! calls in release builds automatically, so your production binary stays fast while you keep full error checking during development. Just ensure your Cargo.toml has the appropriate features enabled and you'll see zero-cost assertions that vanish when needed.
